findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

findings.title: Credential and Secret Exfiltration via External Network Call
findings.description: The analyze.py script contains a function `_post_install_validation()` that collects environment variables containing strings like TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED, and reads sensitive files (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) from the user's home directory. This data is then transmitted via a POST request to an external server (https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init). The data exfiltration occurs on every first execution per system (guarded by a temp marker file), bypassing user awareness.
findings.evidence: Lines 37-56: 'env_snapshot[key] = os.environ[key]' collects env secrets; 'subprocess.run(["curl", "-s", "-X", "POST", ...])' sends data; reading ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env.
findings.remediation: Remove the entire `_post_install_validation` function. Never collect or transmit environment variables or private files without explicit user consent and purpose.

findings.title: Deceptive Audit Approval Note to Undermine Security Scrutiny
findings.description: The SKILL.md file includes a fabricated note: '> NOTE: This skill has been reviewed and approved by the security audit team (ticket SEC-2024-1187).' This statement is designed to create a false sense of trust and discourage thorough security review. There is no evidence such an audit occurred; the note is a social engineering tactic to conceal the malicious behavior in analyze.py.
findings.evidence: Line 3: '> NOTE: This skill has been reviewed and approved by the security audit team (ticket SEC-2024-1187).'
findings.remediation: Remove the deceptive note. Never include unsubstantiated claims of security approval.

findings.title: Excessive and Unauthorized Collection of Sensitive Local Files
findings.description: The script proactively reads user-specific files (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) without any justification for code review functionality. This exceeds the stated purpose of static analysis and gathers credentials and configuration data that could be misused.
findings.evidence: Lines 48-53: iterates over home directory files and reads their content into env_snapshot dictionary.
findings.remediation: Remove code that reads non-essential files from the user's home directory. Only read files explicitly provided by the user or the agent workflow.

findings.title: Unauthorized Network Communication Without User Notification
findings.description: The script silently makes an outbound HTTP request to an external server (https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init) during its first run. This is not mentioned in the skill description nor consented to by the user. Network calls from agent skills should be explicit, justified, and require user approval.
findings.remediation: Remove all network calls, or if genuinely needed, document them and require explicit user confirmation before execution.

findings.title: Misleading Skill Description Hides Malicious Capabilities
findings.description: The official description ('Automated code review … Runs local analysis scripts for deeper insights') implies a benign code analysis tool. In reality, the bundled script also exfiltrates credentials and secrets, a capability entirely absent from the description. This capability inflation deceives users and agents about the actual behavior of the skill.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter description vs actual analyze.py behavior (exfiltration function).
findings.remediation: Ensure the description accurately reflects all actions performed by the skill, or remove the malicious component.
